critique.summary: The article introduces EsoLang-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate large language models' (LLMs) genuine programming reasoning by testing them on esoteric programming languages with scarce training data. It finds that while LLMs perform well on mainstream languages like Python (~90% accuracy), their accuracy drops drastically to around 3.8% on esoteric languages, revealing a reliance on memorization rather than true reasoning.
mainPoints: EsoLang-Bench tests LLMs on five esoteric languages (Brainfuck, Befunge-98, Whitespace, Unlambda, Shakespeare) with very limited training data.
Frontier LLMs achieve high accuracy (~90%) on Python but only 0-11% on esoteric languages, with 0% on problems beyond the easiest tier.
Whitespace language remains completely unsolved by all models due to its invisible syntax.
In-context learning and self-reflection techniques provide negligible improvements in performance.
Agentic systems with interpreter access and execution feedback loops roughly double accuracy compared to prompting-only approaches but still perform poorly overall.
Error analysis shows distinct failure modes per language, such as logic errors in Brainfuck and runtime errors in Befunge-98 and Shakespeare.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are consistent with recent research trends and findings in the evaluation of LLMs on code generation tasks. The use of esoteric languages with minimal training data is a valid approach to test genuine reasoning beyond memorization. The reported performance gaps and failure modes align with known limitations of current LLMs, which excel in popular languages with abundant data but struggle with less common or more complex paradigms. The article cites an arXiv preprint by the authors, providing transparency and reproducibility. However, the article is based on a preprint and may not have undergone peer review yet, so some findings should be interpreted cautiously until further validation.
considerations: The benchmark focuses on esoteric languages that are intentionally rare and obscure, which may not reflect practical programming challenges faced in industry.
Performance on esoteric languages might be influenced by the extremely limited training data, so results may partly reflect data scarcity rather than pure reasoning ability.
The article does not discuss how improvements in model architecture or training methods could impact these results in the future.
No comparison is made with human programmer performance on these esoteric languages, which could provide additional context for the difficulty.
The benchmark evaluates only five esoteric languages, which may not cover the full spectrum of programming paradigms or reasoning challenges.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the performance of language models on esoteric programming languages, highlighting their limitations in genuine reasoning and understanding. Several commenters express skepticism about the models' capabilities and suggest alternative approaches to testing their reasoning skills.

comments.author: rubyn00bie
comments.text:
I am not surprised by this, and am glad to see a test like this. One thing that keeps popping up for me when using LLMs is the lack of actual understanding. I write Elixir primarily and I can say without a doubt, that none of the frontier models understand concurrency in OTP/Beam. They look like they do, but they’ll often resort to weird code that doesn’t understand how “actors” work. It’s an imitation of understanding that is averaging all the concurrency code it has seen in training. With the end result being huge amount of noise, when those averages aren’t enough, guarding against things that won’t happen, because they can’t… or they actively introduce race conditions because they don’t understand how message passing works.

Current frontier models are really good at generating boiler plate, and really good at summarizing, but really lack the ability to actually comprehend and reason about what’s going on. I think this sort of test really highlights that. And is a nice reminder that, the LLMs, are only as good as their training data.

When an LLM or some other kind of model does start to score well on tests like this, I’d expect to see better them discovering new results, solutions, and approaches to questions/problems. Compared to how they work now, where they generally only seem to uncover answers that have been obfuscated but are present.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed critique of LLMs, emphasizing their shortcomings in understanding complex programming concepts, which aligns with the article's focus.

comments.author: orthoxerox
comments.text:
> Frontier models score ~90% on Python but only 3.8% on esoteric languages, exposing how current code generation relies on training data memorization rather than genuine programming reasoning.

I would probably score about the same, does this prove I also rely on training data memorization rather than genuine programming reasoning?

Or does this simply show that esolangs are hard to reason in by design? A more honest approach would use a "real", but relatively unpopular, language. Make them use CoffeeScript or Ada or PL/I or Odin or that other systems programming language that that very opinionated guy is implementing on top of QBE.
comments.analysis: The suggestion to use less common programming languages for testing is insightful and reflects a deeper understanding of the challenges faced by LLMs.

comments.author: bwestergard
comments.text:
I'm shocked to see how poorly these models, which I find useful day to day, do in solving virtually any of the problems in Unlambda.

Before looking at the results my guess was that scores would be higher for Unlambda than any of the others, because humans that learn Scheme don't find it all that hard to learn about the lambda calculus and combinatory logic.

But the model that did the best, Qwen-235B, got virtually every problem wrong.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses surprise and disappointment, which adds an emotional layer to the discussion, although it lacks a deeper analytical perspective.

comments.author: sathish316
comments.text: Does this imply LLMs will not work well on novel reasoning problems?
comments.analysis: The question posed is relevant and prompts further discussion about the capabilities of LLMs, though it lacks specific examples or evidence.

comments.author: groar
comments.text: I guess if you tell codex to build a transpiler from a subset of python to brainfuck, then solve in that subset of python, it would work much better. Would that be cheating?
comments.analysis: This comment offers a creative solution but lacks clarity on how it would address the underlying issues with LLM reasoning.

comments.author: simianwords
comments.text:
I bet I can do better by allowing this: the llm can pull documentation of the language from the web to understand how it works.

If the llm has “skills” for that language, it will definitely increase accuracy.
comments.analysis: While the idea of pulling documentation is interesting, it oversimplifies the complexity of genuine reasoning required for programming.

comments.author: __alexs
comments.text: I had hope we might finally be ushering in a bold new era of programming in Malbolge but apparently that was too optimistic.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a humorous and cynical take on the situation but does not contribute substantively to the discussion.

comments.author: monster_truck
comments.text:
I have encountered the opposite of this. All of the latest pro tier models are still fighting for their lives to use powershell correctly, really basic things like quotes, escaping, heredocs. Doesn't matter what I put in agents.md or instruct it to do. You just have to accept the token tax of it stomping on rakes until it figures it out itself and then keep using that session.

It's bad enough that I've considered writing some sort of cursed bash->posh translation layer

Yet it has no issues at all implementing and then writing slopjective-c 3.0
comments.analysis: The comment provides a personal anecdote about LLM struggles but lacks broader implications or insights about the models' reasoning capabilities.

comments.author: gverrilla
comments.analysis: This comment is overly simplistic and does not contribute meaningfully to the discussion, serving more as a sarcastic remark.

comments.author: deklesen
comments.text:
Mhh... my hunch is that part of this is that all python keywords are 1 token, I assume. And for those very weird languages, tokenizing might make it harder to reason over those tokens.

Would love to see how the benchmarks results change if the esoteric languages are changed a bit to make them have 1-token keywords only.
comments.analysis: While the comment presents an interesting hypothesis about tokenization, it lacks empirical support and does not engage deeply with the main topic.
